title: 第 57 節
en: Now begins the hymn to Durga, though there is as yet nothing in these verses that points directly to this goddess as described in the Markant/eya Pura,na. AVe have had the epithets siva and durga in the preceding verses, as applied to Eatri, we now see her called Durga, but without any definite mythological character.

' I shall eagerly praise the divine (devi) Durga, who yields a refuge, who is beloved by the Bahvrz^-as (priests of the Kig-veda), who is equal to a thousand \ Let us pour out Soma to 6^atavedas (Fire) ^.
'Thou art ai:)proached ^ by i?ishis for the peace of the twice-born, thou art born in the Rig-veda. May she (or Agni) burn uj^ the wealth of the enemy.
'Whatever Brahmanas, learned or ignorant, approach thee, 0 Goddess (devi), the carrier of oblations, may she (or he) carry us over all obstacles (durgam).
' Whatever twice-born men shall celebrate the fire-coloured, beautiful, gentle (goddess), she will carry them over obstacles, — Agni (carries) across all evils, as in a boat across a river.

' All who are bewildered in obstacles, in misfortune, in fearful war, in trouble from enemies, in visitations from fire or thieves, in escape from evil stars, in troublesome obstacles, in wars and wildernesses, approach thee. Give us security from these, give us security from these. Om, adoration !

' The long-haired (kesini), whose name among all creatures is Pa?i^ami (the Fifth, or Beautiful), may she, the good Night, the goddess (devi), preserve us always, may she preserve us always. Om, adoration !
^ Could it be sahasra samgnitam, with a thousand names ? ^ The last lines in this and the three following verses are borrowed from Rv. I. 99. ^ Samupasrita.

'I approach as my refuge Durga, the goddess, the firecoloured, flaming with heat, the daughter of the sun (Virok&nd), who is welcome for the rewards of good works. O thou well-speeding, adoration to thy speed !
' May Durga, the goddess (deviA), be propitious for our success !

' He who always recites this praise of Durga night after night [viz. a ratri, Kusika Saubhara, the poet, or Ratri Bharadv%i, praise of the Night, metre Gayatri], he who mutters the hymn to Eatri, he succeeds at that very time.'

This is clearly a compilation made on purpose for a goddess Durga. What we can recognise behind her, are the night and the fire. The epithet durga, difficult to pass, or difficult to approach, would be applicable to both.
In the Taittiriya-ara?iyaka, X. 7, we find a verse addressed to the same goddess, but under the name of DurgiA :
Katyayanaya vidmahe, Kanyakumari dhimahi, tan no Durgi/i proZ:odayat.

I doubt whether the text is correct. "We expect the names Katyayani, the accusative Kanyakumarim, and Durga. The commentator, however, explains Durgi for Durga as lingavyatyayas ^7iandasaA, and it has to be observed that all the preceding deities are masculine, namely, Rudra, Vinayaka, Nandi, Kartikeya, GarucZa, Brahma, Vishnu, Narasimha, Aditya, Agni, and lastly Durgi. In the text of the Mahanarayana Upanishad, III. 12 (ed. G. A. Jacob, 1888, p. 4), the text is more correct :
Katyayanyai vidmahe, Kanyakumarim dhimahi, tan no Durga prakadayat.

Again, in Taitt.-Ar. X. 26 and 30, we find invocations addressed to Gayatri, which strongly remind us of Durga. In the 26th Anuvaka, Gayatri is called varada, boon-giving, and in the 30th Anuvaka she is spoken of as uttame sikhare ^ata (or devi) bhumyjim parvata-murdhani, 'born on the highest peak, on the earth, on the summit of the mountain,'
ANTHROPOLOGICAL EVIDENCE. 413 which epithets might refer to Parvati, but are by tradition referred to Gayatri or Sarasvati.
Several of the names given to Eudra in the TaittiriyaAranyaka, point to him as the liusband of Durga. Thus we read, X. 18:
Namo hiranyabahave, hiraTiyavarwaya, hirawyarupaya, hiranyapataye, Ambikapataye, Umapataye, Pasupataye, namo nama^.
'Adoration to the golden-armed, golden-coloured, goldenshaped, the lord of gold, the lord of Ambika, the lord of Uma, the lord of cattle ! '

This Ambika is mentioned as the sister of Piudra in V%. Samh. III. 57, and his name Tryambaka is derived from stri-ambika (ASatap.-Br. II. 6, 2, 9), because Ambika, his sister, shared the sacrifice with him. This Ambika is in Taitt.-Br. I. 6, 10, 4, identified with ^S'arad, the autumn.

The name Tryambaka has also been explained as ' having three mothers, or sisters.' But this can hardly be, as his wife also is called Tryambaka. Most likely it was meant as another expression for Trilo^ana, three-eyed, one of the most general names of iSiva in later times.

As it has been suggested that my representation of the untrustworthy character of much of the evidence on which students of comparative theology and of anthropology have to rely is exaggerated, I give here some of the ^neces justificatives. I do not blame anybody. On the contrary, I highly appreciate the labours of those who have given us their accounts of what they have seen and heard among savage 414 APPENDIX V.

tribes. Nor have I any right to find fault with others who in their study of language, customs, and religions have trusted these accounts, as I myself have but too often shared their fate. All I say is that now that we have found out by sad experience how untrustworthy some of these accounts are, and how often they flatly contradict each other, we ought to discard all evidence that does not come to us either from a man who was able himself to converse with native races, or who was at least an eye-witness of what he relates. Even then, I know full well, there still remain many dangers. Savages, when brought into conversational intercourse with white people, are generally very anxious to please. They never like to disappoint or contradict their questioners. Mr. Ellis in his charming Polynesian Researches, published many years ago, remarks that almost in the same breath a Malagasy will express his belief that when he dies he ceases altogether to exist, and yet confess the fact that he is in the habit of praying to his ancestors who are supposed to hover about their tombs.

It was Darwin, I believe, who remarked very truly, ' that the effects of false inferences are but of little moment, for every one feels a pleasure in setting them straight, but that false facts are most dangerous, because there may be but few who can point out their untruth^.' When we have to deal with the evidence taken from language or from literary works, everybody can form his own opinion, or, if he cannot, he can abstain. But when we have to deal with evidence even of eyewitnesses from the Andaman Islands, and if these eye-witnesses not only differ, but contradict each other, what shall we do ? Shall we go to the Andaman Islands, before finishing a sentence ? And if we did go, would it not take years before we could learn the native dialects in order to be able freely to converse with the natives, and thus to guard ANTHROPOLOGICAL EVIDENCE. 415 against the very mishaps that have befallen those who came before us 1 All we can do is to follow strictly the two principles which I laid down in my Lecture, never to quote any but eye-witnesses, and never to trust even eye-witnesses unless they were familiar with the language. This, no doubt, will very considerably reduce the bulk of what has been written on anecdotic anthropology, and more particularly, on comparative theology, but what we lose in quantity we shall certainly gain in quality.

It is unfortunate that this subject cannot be discussed without exciting personal resentments. I sometimes wish that all learned works could be written anonymously. Whenever one scholar arrives at results different from those hitherto accepted, it is looked upon as a kind of slur thrown on those who hold the old opinion. It does not seem to be so in other sciences. If a new planet is discovered by one observer, the other astronomers do not consider themselves disgraced, or bound in honour to defend their old map of the stars. When spectral analysis showed that former theories on the constituent elements of the sun were imperfect and erroneous, no one felt ashamed of his former ignorance.

Why should it be different with scholars ? The difficulty of gaining a clear conception of a religion by addressing some questions on religious topics to a few savages is enormous. It is not easy, as we all know, to draw an intelligent answer on the most common subjects from a ploughboy. But a native of Australia stands on a much lower level than most English ploughboys. And if he is asked any question by a white man, he is frightened. He does not know why he should be asked such questions, and he is at once afraid of mischief. When he gives an answer, the questioner himself is often at a loss to understand, or, thinking he does understand it, he entirely misapprehends its real meaning.
Names of Places.

What can be simpler than to ask the name of a place 1 But though wandering tribes may have names for any remarkable spot in their territories, they seldom feel the necessity of naming larger areas, and when they are asked their names, they are as much at a loss as if we were asked the name of a long extent of downs or forests. Thus it is extremely probable that the name of what is now called the Manera Flams arose from a Sydney black being asked the name, and replying manyer, that is, / do not know. Ever since the place has been called Manera.
zh: 現在開始了對杜爾迦的讚頌詩，雖然在這些詩句中尚未出現任何直接指向《馬爾干德亞往世書》（Markant/eya Pura,na）中所描述之此女神的內容。我們在前面的詩句中已見到「濕婆」（siva）和「杜爾迦」（durga）這些稱號被用來稱呼婆底妮，而今我們看到她被稱為杜爾迦，但尚無任何明確的神話學特徵。
我將熱切讚頌神聖的（devi）杜爾迦，她賜予庇護，為巴赫弗里（Bahvrz^-as）（梨俱吠陀的祭司）所熱愛，其功德等同於千般。讓我們向沙塔韋達斯（Satavedas）（火）奉獻蘇摩酒^。
「汝為仙人（rishis）所親近，以求庇護二度生者之安寧；汝生於梨俱吠陀（Rig-veda）。願她（或阿耆尼）焚毀敵人之財富。」
「無論博學或無知的婆羅門（Brahmanas）如何親近妳，啊，女神（devi），那位獻祭的承載者，願她（或他）將我們載越一切障礙（durgam）。」
『凡是兩度重生之人，若讚美火紅、美麗、溫柔之（女神），她必將承載他們跨越障礙，——阿耆尼（Agni）如同舟楫渡河一般，載人跨越一切邪惡。』
凡被障礙所惑、被不幸所困、在可怕的戰爭中、因敵人而受苦難、因火焰或盜賊的折磨、逃離惡星的威脅、遭逢麻煩的障礙、在戰爭與曠野中者，皆趨近於汝。賜予我等免於此等難的保護，賜予我等免於此等難的保護。唵，致敬之禮！
「蓬髮者（kesini），其名在眾生中為帕奇亞彌（第五者，或美麗者），願她，善良的夜晚，女神（devi），永遠保佑我們，永遠保佑我們。唵，敬禮！
^ 難道是 sahasra samgnitam，具有千個名字嗎？^ 本詩及其後三首詩歌的最後數行取自《梨俱吠陀》I. 99。^ Samupasrita。
我投靠杜爾迦（Durga）為我的庇護所，她是女神，火紅之色，熾熱烈焰，太陽之女（Virokanda），因善業之果報而備受歡迎。噫！疾速者啊，恭敬於汝之速疾！
願杜爾迦（devi）女神恩待我們的成功！
「夜復一夜誦讀杜爾迦讚歌者[即夜讚（ratri）、庫西卡‧索巴拉詩人之作，或《夜讚》（Ratri Bharadv%i）、讚美夜晚，韻律為迦雅特里（Gayatri）], 誦唸厄特里讚歌者，彼即於彼時成就。」
這顯然是專為女神杜爾迦（Durga）而編纂的文獻。在她的形象背後，我們能辨識出的是夜晚與火焰。上述詞彙「杜爾迦」（durga），意為難以逾越或難以接近，既適用於夜晚，亦適用於火焰。
在《泰帝利亞‧阿蘭亞卡》（Taittiriya-ara?iyaka）第十卷第七章，我們發現了一段獻給同一位女神的詩句，但在此處她以杜爾迦（DurgiA）之名示人：
我注意到提供的原文並非英文，而是梵文（Devanagari字母的拉丁轉寫）。這是一首梵文頌禱文（護摩禱詞），而非穆勒《宗教學導論》中的英文文本。 根據您的翻譯規則第2條，我只應翻譯英文原文。請提供實際的英文原文片段，我將按照規則準確譯為繁體中文。

我對文本的正確性表示懷疑。「我們期待看到 Katyayani 名字、賓格 Kanyakumarim，以及 Durga。然而注疏者卻將 Durgi 解釋為 Durga 作為 lingavyatyayas （語法變異），並且必須留意到所有前述的提婆都是陽性，即 Rudra、Vinayaka、Nandi、Kartikeya、Garuda、Brahma、Vishnu、Narasimha、Aditya、Agni，最後是 Durgi。在《摩訶那羅延奧義書》III. 12（G. A. Jacob 編，1888 年，第 4 頁）的文本中，文本更為準確：
我們知曉迦帝耶衍尼（Katyayanyai vidmahe），讓我們冥想少女之神（Kanyakumarim dhimahi），願杜爾迦（Durga）為我們顯現光明（prakadayat）。

再者，在《泰帝利耶‧阿蘭雅卡》X. 26 和 30 中，我們發現了對迦耶特里（Gayatri）的祈禱文，其內容強烈地讓我們想起杜爾迦。在第 26 個阿努瓦卡（Anuvaka）中，迦耶特里被稱為瓦拉達（varada），即賜福者；在第 30 個阿努瓦卡中，她被描述為烏塔梅‧錫卡雷（uttame sikhare）〔或提毘（devi）〕布慕吉（bhumyjim）帕瓦塔‧木爾達尼（parvata-murdhani），意為「生於最高峰，在大地之上，於山岳之巔」。
人類學證據。413 這些上號（epithets）可能指涉帕瓦蒂（Parvati），但按傳統則被歸屬於蓋亞特里（Gayatri）或莎拉斯瓦蒂（Sarasvati）。
《泰帝利耶林書》(Taittiriya Aranyaka) 中賦予魯陀(Rudra)的幾個名號，都指向他是杜爾迦(Durga)的丈夫。我們在第十卷第十八章讀到如下記載：
敬禮金臂者、金色持有者、金色形體者、金色之主、安比卡之主、烏摩之主、畜主（Pasupataye），恭敬敬禮、恭敬敬禮。
「頂禮於金臂、金色、金形、黃金之主、安比卡之主、烏摩之主、牲畜之主！」

這位安彼迦（Ambika）在《梨俱吠陀》第三卷第五十七頌（V%. Samh. III. 57）中被提及為皮烏德拉的妹妹，其名字特里安巴卡（Tryambaka）衍生自梵文「女性‧安彼迦」（stri-ambika），見於《舌蒂跋多梵書》第二卷第六部分第二、九節（ASatap.-Br. II. 6, 2, 9），因為安彼迦是他的妹妹，與他共同主持祭祀。這位安彼迦在《泰迪利亞梵書》第一卷第六部分第十、四節（Taitt.-Br. I. 6, 10, 4）中，被認同為莎拉德（^S'arad），即秋季。
試安巴卡（Tryambaka）這個名字也曾被解釋為「有三位母親或姐妹」。但這幾乎不太可能，因為他的妻子也被稱為試安巴卡。最有可能的是，它意在表達三眼神（Trilorana）的另一種說法，這是濕婆神（Śiva）在後世最常見的名號之一。
既然有人認為我對於比較神學與人類學研究者必須依憑的大量證據不可信之特性的陳述是言過其實，我在此提供若干必要的佐證。我並非要指責任何人。相反地，我極其推崇那些將其在野蠻民族中所見所聞紀錄下來的學者的勞績。

部落民族。我也沒有任何權利去指責其他人在研究語言、風俗和宗教時信任這些記載，因為我自己也不免多次遭遇同樣的厄運。我所說的只是，既然我們已經透過痛苦的經驗發現某些這類記載多麼不可信，以及它們如何經常彼此直接矛盾，我們應當捨棄所有不是來自於某個曾經能夠親自與原住民族進行對話的人，或至少親眼見證過他所述說之事的人的證據。即使是這樣，我深知仍然存有許多危險。野蠻人一旦與白人進入對話交往，通常都非常渴望取悅他人。他們絕不願意令提問者失望或與之相悖。埃利斯先生在他那部多年前出版的迷人著作《波利尼西亞研究》(Polynesian Researches) 中評述道，一位馬達加斯加人幾乎同時會表達他相信死後將完全不復存在的信念，然而又承認他習慣於向據稱在其墳墓周圍徘徊的祖先祈禱這一事實。

我相信達爾文曾精辟地指出：「虛假的推論所造成的影響微乎其微，因為每個人都樂於將其糾正，但虛假的事實最為危險，因為可能只有少數人能指出其不實之處^。」當我們必須處理從語言或文學作品中取得的證據時，每個人都能形成自己的見解，或者如果不能，也可以保持沉默。但當我們必須處理來自安達曼群島目擊者的證據，而且這些目擊者不僅意見相左，更相互矛盾時，我們該如何是好？我們是否必須在完成一個句子之前就前往安達曼群島？而且即使我們確實前往了，是否不得不花費數年時間才能學會當地方言，以便能與當地居民自由交談，從而防止已降臨在先人身上的種種不幸？我們所能做的只是嚴格遵循我在講座中所闡述的兩項原則：決不引用除目擊者之外的證詞，即便信任目擊者也決不能無條件地信任，除非他們熟悉該語言。毫無疑問，這將大幅減少迄今為止在軼聞人類學領域所著述的作品數量，尤其是在比較神學領域，但我們在數量上所失的，必將在品質上獲得補償。

不幸的是，這個主題無法不引起個人的反感而加以討論。我有時希望所有的學術著作都能匿名發表。每當某位學者的研究結果與迄今為止公認的觀點不同時，就會被視為對持舊說者的一種詆毀。這在其他科學領域中似乎並非如此。當一位觀測者發現一顆新行星時，其他天文學家並不會認為自己蒙受恥辱，也不會感到有榮譽上的義務去維護他們舊日的星圖。當分光分析證明了關於太陽組成元素的舊有理論是不完善且錯誤的時，沒有人會因為自己從前的無知而感到羞愧。

為什麼學者之間應該有所不同呢？透過向幾位未開化民族提出宗教議題的問題來獲得某一宗教的清晰概念，其困難程度是巨大的。眾所周知，即使在最平常的主題上，要從農家男孩那裡得到一個聰慧的回答也並非易事。但澳洲土著的文明程度遠低於大多數英國農家男孩。如果他被白人提出任何問題，他會感到驚恐。他不知道為什麼會被提出這樣的問題，他立即擔心會有什麼不幸。當他給出回答時，提問者本身往往無法理解，或者即使自認為理解了，也完全誤解了其真實的含義。
地名
問一個地方的名字再簡單不過了。但雖然遊牧民族可能為其領地內任何顯著的地點命名，他們卻很少感到有必要為較大的區域命名。當被問及這些區域的名稱時，他們就像被問及一片長長的丘陵或森林的名稱一樣不知所措。因此，現在被稱為曼內拉河（Manera Flams）的名稱極有可能起源於一位悉尼土著被問及其名稱，並回答「manyer」——也就是「我不知道」——這樣的情形。從那時起，這個地方就一直被稱為曼內拉。
